title: Overwhelmed with honor and joy

audience_match: Friends, colleagues, and acquaintances of the Panganiban-Prieto-Gesmundo circle
Editorial staff and supporting team members at the Inquirer

primary_topics: Launch of 'With Due Respect 3' â€” third five-year compilation of CJ's Inquirer columns
CJ's account of how he was persuaded to write a column by Marixi R. Prieto despite his initial unworthiness
Tribute to incumbent CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo who launched the book
Recognition of the supporting team (JV Rufino, Sean James Borja, Inquirer editors)
sub_topics: Marixi R. Prieto and daughter Sandy Prieto-Romualdez (Inquirer Group President and CEO) as publishers of the every-five-years compilations (2011, 2016, 2021)
CJ's youthful newspaper-peddling in Sampaloc, Manila â€” never dreamt of writing a column
Dr. Jovito R. Salonga's warning: 'Chief Justices make history; they do not write it'
Marixi's rejoinder: 'Dr. Salonga may be correct, but I do not know of any retired chief justice who had been offered to write a column'
15-year column career (Feb 2007â€“2022), longer than 11 years on the Supreme Court â€” every Sunday without fail despite illnesses, travel, 25+ corporate engagements
WDR1 ranked third on Amazon bestseller list â€” bested only by Jeffrey Toobin (CNN) at #1 and Antonin Scalia (US SC) at #2
CJ Gesmundo at 65 â€” 'he could be my son, and I could be his father'; named without seeking it; low public profile; the four constitutional attributes (competence, integrity, probity, independence)
Gesmundo as 'glutton for work,' determined to solve perennial backlog, familiar with new technologies, ready to automate the judiciary
JV Rufino (Inquirer Book Company), Sean James Borja (Ateneo valedictorian 2018, 2018 bar exam first placer) arranging columns topically

stances.claim: CJ owes his 15-year column career â€” longer than his 11+ years on the Supreme Court â€” to Marixi R. Prieto's persistent persuasion, against Dr. Jovito R. Salonga's warning that 'Chief Justices make history; they do not write it,' which Marixi answered with 'Dr. Salonga may be correct, but I do not know of any retired chief justice who had been offered to write a column.'
stances.rhetorical_move: gratitude-attribution narrative grounded in mentor-vs-publisher dialogue

stances.claim: CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo, at 65 young enough to be CJ Panganiban's son, embodies the four constitutional attributes required of jurists â€” proven competence, integrity, probity, and independence â€” and is 'a glutton for work' determined to solve the perennial backlog and automate the judiciary using new technologies he is already familiar with.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional-criteria endorsement framework (Article VIII attributes as evaluation rubric)

stances.claim: The success of the WDR series â€” WDR1 placing third on the Amazon bestseller list, topped only by Jeffrey Toobin (CNN) at #1 and Antonin Scalia at #2 â€” reflects sustained reader engagement that CJ attributes to the publishing team's care rather than his own merit.

notable_anecdotes.summary: CJ's youthful newspaper-peddling 'for many years in the streets of Sampaloc, Manila, to help my impoverished family meet our daily sustenance when I was an elementary and high school student' â€” 'a historical romance with newspapers' that anticipated by decades his eventual columnist career.

Dr. Jovito R. Salonga's institutional taboo: 'Chief Justices make history; they do not write it' â€” and Marixi's deflating one-liner answer: 'I do not know of any retired chief justice who had been offered to write a column.'
WDR1 placing third on Amazon's bestseller list, bested only by Jeffrey Toobin of CNN at first and Antonin Scalia at second â€” Panganiban placed in genuinely elite company.

The 15-year column writing streak: 'every Sunday without fail, despite occasional illnesses, travel abroad about five times a year, and pressing work as an officer, corporate director, or adviser in over 25 companies and foundations.'
CJ Gesmundo at age 65 placed in the father-son relation to CJ Panganiban â€” 'he could be my son, and I could be his father' â€” capturing the institutional generational handover.
Sean James Borja's double-distinction: Ateneo de Manila law class of 2018 valedictorian and 1st place in that year's bar exam â€” a parenthetical credentialing flourish for the column's editor.

decision_framework_signals: uses constitutional Article VIII attributes (competence, integrity, probity, independence) as endorsement rubric for jurists
treats reader-market success (Amazon ranking) as validation worth crediting outward rather than inward
anchors gratitude in specific-mentor and specific-publisher attribution rather than vague
uses generational-relation framing (father/son age gap) to mark institutional continuity
credentialing-density as legitimation device for younger associates (Borja's bar and valedictorian honors)

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban reproduces his response speech at the December 2021 launch of 'With Due Respect 3,' the third five-year compilation of his Inquirer columns published by Marixi R. Prieto and her daughter Sandy Prieto-Romualdez (Inquirer Group President and CEO). He recounts how, despite a youthful 'historical romance with newspapers' as a Sampaloc paper-peddler, he had never written a column until after retiring from the judiciary and was very hesitant when Marixi invited him â€” especially after Dr. Jovito R. Salonga told him 'Chief Justices make history; they do not write it,' to which Marixi answered, 'Dr. Salonga may be correct, but I do not know of any retired chief justice who had been offered to write a column.' He has written every Sunday for 15 years since February 2007 â€” longer than his 11+ years on the Court â€” and credits Marixi for whatever accolades have followed, including WDR1's third place on Amazon's bestseller list (topped only by Jeffrey Toobin at #1 and Antonin Scalia at #2). He thanks incumbent CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo for launching WDR3 â€” Gesmundo at 65 could be CJ Panganiban's son â€” and endorses him as embodying the four constitutional attributes (competence, integrity, probity, independence), 'a glutton for work' determined to solve the perennial SC backlog and automate the judiciary. He closes by thanking JV Rufino of the Inquirer Book Company; 'CompaÃ±ero' Sean James Borja (Ateneo 2018 valedictorian and 2018 bar first placer) who arranged the columns topically rather than chronologically; and the editors Jorge Aruta, Rosario Garcellano (retired), and Gilbert Cadiz (incumbent opinion editor) for patience with his grammatical lapses.
